summary: Gruber reports on Apple's Security Update 2004-06-07, which closes all the URI/Launch Services vulnerabilities that had been publicized over the previous month. He praises the update for being well-documented, noting it meets the standard he called for in his earlier piece 'Security Cannot Be Spun.' The fix works by presenting a confirmation dialog when Launch Services tries to automatically launch an app the user has never manually opened. Gruber updates his own vulnerability instructions and notes that while the known holes are patched, he still recommends leaving Safari's 'Open safe files' preference disabled as a precaution.
key_points: Apple's security update closes every known URI/Launch Services vulnerability from the preceding month
The release notes are praised as genuinely well-documented, a marked improvement over Apple's usual security communication
Apple's solution adds a confirmation dialog for auto-launching unknown apps, fixing the vulnerability without removing functionality
The 'disk' and 'disks' URI protocols have been completely removed rather than merely patched
Gruber recommends keeping Safari's 'Open safe files' preference off despite the fixes, arguing that no automatically opened file should be considered truly safe
money_quotes: It's not just better-documented than usual, it's just flat-out well-documented.
This solves the vulnerability where unknown apps could be launched automatically, and does so without removing any functionality.
I think one of the lessons of this saga has been that it's unwise to think there exists such a thing as a 'safe file' that can be opened automatically after it's been downloaded.
In the spirit of 'better safe than sorry', I'm leaving this preference off.
Documentation like this is exactly what I wished for in 'Security Cannot Be Spun'.
key_insight: Apple delivered a well-executed, well-documented security fix that closed every known vulnerability without sacrificing functionality, though the episode proved that trusting any file type as automatically 'safe' is fundamentally unwise.
